coredata.dc:title: Acoustic cavitation promoted lipase catalysed synthesis of isobutyl propionate in solvent free system: Optimization and kinetic studies
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, Kajal S.
Rathod, Virendra K.
coredata.dc:description:
The present work highlights the effect of ultrasound on enzymatic synthesis of isobutyl propionate, a rum flavor by esterification of isobutanol and propionic acid in non-aqueous, solvent free system (SFS) using Fermase CALB™10000. The optimization study for different variables in presence of ultrasound showed a maximum conversion of 95.14% at 60°C temperature, 4% w/w enzyme dose, 1:3 acid:alcohol ratio, 40W power, 25kHz frequency, 50% duty cycle and 150rpm speed in 3h as compared to 10h of conventional method. The optimal enzyme loading was reduced to 4% w/w using ultrasound irradiation compared to 5% w/w of conventional. The efficiency of enzyme improved notably and can be reused up to seven cycles preserving its former activity. The application of ultrasound greatly enhanced esterification reactions by maintaining enzyme stability and improving the production yield. Moreover, owing towards a green approach, the synthesis is carried out in SFS for development of lucrative flavor ester. Bisubstrate kinetic models like random bi-bi, ping pong bi-bi and ordered bi-bi were applied to the experimental data using non-linear regression analysis. The experimental data and kinetic study revealed that reaction obeyed Ping-Pong bi-bi model with kinetic parameters, Vmax
=50.0M/min/gcatalyst, KA
=4.87×10−2 M, KB
=9.06×10−4 M, KiA
=9.8×10−1 M, KiB
=1.05×10−3 M & SSE =2.74×10−4 for lipase catalysed synthesis of isobutyl propionate under ultrasound with inhibition by both acid and alcohol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Isobutyl propionate
Ultrasound
Rum fruity flavor
Esterification
Lipase
Biocatalysis
Kinetic study

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of ultrasonic irradiation v/s conventional stirring method

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of temperature on conversion (A) and initial rate vs. temperature (B). Reaction condition: time – 3h, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz, speed of agitation – 150rpm & duty cycle – 50%.

Effect of enzyme loading on conversion (A) and initial rate vs. Enzyme loading (B). Reaction condition: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz, speed of agitation – 150rpm & duty cycle – 50%.

Effect of molar ratio of propionic acid and isobutanol on conversion. Reaction condition: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz, speed of agitation – 150rpm & duty cycle – 50%.

Effect of agitation speed on conversion (A) and initial rate vs. Speed of agitation (B). Reaction condition: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz & duty cycle – 50%.

Effect of duty cycle input on conversion (A) and initial rate vs. Duty cycle (B). Reaction condition: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz &speed of agitation – 150rpm.

Effect of power (A), initial rate vs. power (B) and Effect of frequency (C) of ultrasound irradiation on conversion. Reaction condition: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, speed of agitation – 150rpm & duty cycle – 50%.

Reusability study of lipase for isobutyl propionate synthesis. Reaction conditions: time – 3h, temperature – 60°C, enzyme dose – 4% w/w, propionic acid: isobutyl alcohol ratio – 1:3, ultrasound power – 40W, ultrasound frequency – 25kHz, duty cycle 50% & agitation speed – 150rpm.
Comparison between ultrasound irradiation with stirring, conventional stirring method, ultrasound irradiation without stirring.
Parity plot of predicted rate vs. experimental rate (A) and comparison of initial rate vs. Acid concentration in case of all three models with experimental results (B).
Schematic representation of binary complex mechanism.
Comparison between ultrasound irradiation with stirring, conventional stirring method, ultrasound irradiation without stirring.
Comparison table of kinetic parameter values for the isobutyl propionate synthesis.
Range of reaction condition and optimised reaction parameters for isobutyl propionate synthesis.

head.ce:title: Acoustic cavitation promoted lipase catalysed synthesis of isobutyl propionate in solvent free system: Optimization and kinetic studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Institute of Chemical Technology, Matunga (E), Mumbai 400019, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The present work highlights the effect of ultrasound on enzymatic synthesis of isobutyl propionate, a rum flavor by esterification of isobutanol and propionic acid in non-aqueous, solvent free system (SFS) using Fermase CALB™10000. The optimization study for different variables in presence of ultrasound showed a maximum conversion of 95.14% at 60°C temperature, 4% w/w enzyme dose, 1:3 acid:alcohol ratio, 40W power, 25kHz frequency, 50% duty cycle and 150rpm speed in 3h as compared to 10h of conventional method. The optimal enzyme loading was reduced to 4% w/w using ultrasound irradiation compared to 5% w/w of conventional. The efficiency of enzyme improved notably and can be reused up to seven cycles preserving its former activity. The application of ultrasound greatly enhanced esterification reactions by maintaining enzyme stability and improving the production yield. Moreover, owing towards a green approach, the synthesis is carried out in SFS for development of lucrative flavor ester. Bisubstrate kinetic models like random bi-bi, ping pong bi-bi and ordered bi-bi were applied to the experimental data using non-linear regression analysis. The experimental data and kinetic study revealed that reaction obeyed Ping-Pong bi-bi model with kinetic parameters, Vmax
=50.0M/min/gcatalyst, KA
=4.87×10−2 M, KB
=9.06×10−4 M, KiA
=9.8×10−1 M, KiB
=1.05×10−3 M & SSE =2.74×10−4 for lipase catalysed synthesis of isobutyl propionate under ultrasound with inhibition by both acid and alcohol.

ce:section.ce:para: Isobutyl propionate, a short chain ester is broadly allocated having a wide range of applications in food and flavor industry. It is an ester having rum like fruity odor that adds essence to the beverages, candies, baked goods and procure as an ideal flavor in food industry. A desirable amount of enzyme based products have been commercialized and the commercial market for food flavor industry is profoundly increasing. Isobutyl propionate can be synthesized by different conventional chemical catalysis approaches as reported in the literature such as, esterification of propionic acid and isobutanol in the presence of Amberlyst-15 as a catalyst [1]. Isobutyl propionate synthesis from propionic acid and isobutyl alcohol employing SO42-/TiO2/La3+as catalyst using cyclohexane solvent has also been reported [2]. The conventional esterification reactions have certain drawbacks such as high temperature and pressure, prolonged reaction time, extreme reaction condition, handle of high risk chemicals, decreased selectivity and elevated activation of energy (Ea). Alternatively, esters can be extracted naturally from plant sources, but these methods are highly expensive [3]. Furthermore, the wide industrial demand for green technologies owing to environmental issue has greatly encouraged researchers to develop more environment friendly route for biocatalytic synthesis of esters.

In this regard, enzyme biocatalysis which recommend the use of natural enzyme has gained noticeable attention for the production of flavors [4]. Accordingly, the application of enzymes has been expanded through green technology processes to synthesise flavors with favorable reaction conditions. The products synthesised by employing biocatalysis are more pure than chemical catalysis enabling improvement with the regio-specificity and stereo specificity [5]. Precisely, lipase is the proficient enzyme catalyst and collective knowledge on enzymes has extended its application due to its broad substrate array. A significant focus of researchers is directed towards biocatalysis and to explore newly emerged technologies. Recently, the major shift has occurred towards the production of flavor esters in a solvent free system (SFS); the absence of solvents facilitates downstream processing, thus reduces overall production cost and minimizes environmental impact. However, the solvent free system for lipase catalysed synthesis of flavor ester has been seldom explored and a very few documents are available in the literature [6]. Moreover, biocatalysis favored over chemical catalysis as immobilization of enzyme may hinder the rate of diffusion of substrate to the active catalytic site [7]. Researchers have overcome the shortcomings by replacing the traditional catalysis method through development and use of newly emerged ultrasound irradiation technology for process intensification [8–10]. Ultrasound waves travel through a liquid medium and cause cavitation in the bulk which leads to increasing synergy between phases in a system. The cavitation effect enables the formation of micro bubbles in the medium and the micro bubbles implodes violently with extreme temperature and pressure. The intense stream of liquid disrupts the boundary to create violent shock waves that propagates through liquid which cause emulsification and intense turbulence. Thus, ultrasound technique is considered to surpass the mass transfer limitation by providing sufficient activation energy to trigger reaction rates [11,12]. Moreover, ultrasound is accounted as greener technology and made remarkable success in biocatalysis due to its high-caliber efficiency, economic execution, low cost input and safe reuse [13,14]. Recently, ultrasound has become proven technology in enzyme catalysed production of esters in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics and food industries.

In the present protocol, we herein report the effect of ultrasound irradiation on enzyme catalysed synthesis of isobutyl propionate from isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid catalysed by immobilized Candida antarctica lipase B in SFS. The traditional approach for bioconversion of isobutyl propionate using lipase B reached equilibrium in 10h at optimized conditions [15]. In view of this, the aim of work is to lower the process time of reaction and enhance the productivity in presence of ultrasound irradiation over traditional stirring method.

The enzyme used was Fermase CALB™10000, a commercial Candida antarctica lipase B immobilised on polyacrylate beads (particle size: 300–500lm>95.0%) was provided by Fermenta Biotech Ltd., Mumbai. The supposed activity of the catalytic composition was NLT10000 PLU units per g dry weight (Propyllaurate synthesis). One PLU unit is defined as the amount of enzyme required to consume 1micromole of lauric acid per min at 60°C under the optimized experimental condition. Isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid (purity>98%), ethanol (AR) grade, sodium carbonate (AR) grade, sodium hydroxide (AR) grade, potassium hydroxide (AR) grade, Phenolphthalein (AR) grade, were purchased from Thomas Baker (Chemicals) Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai. Molecular sieves (4Å) were purchased from M/s Merck Ltd., Mumbai.

The conventional enzymatic synthesis of isobutyl propionate was performed with the same reaction condition as reported earlier [15]. As per the process, experiment was performed in a glass reactor of 50mL capacity employed with turbine impeller for stirring. The reactor was place in a water bath and the temperature of water bath can be controlled by temperature control system. The required quantities of reactants were added in the reactor assembly and the reaction volume was maintained at 15mL. The reaction mixture was agitated at a speed of 300rpm at 60°C for few minutes. Enzyme was added along with continuous stirring to initiate the reaction. Small amount of samples were withdrawn periodically (10min) for analysis.

The enzyme catalysed ultrasound assisted esterification reaction was carried out using an ultrasonic thermostatic water bath (Model No. 6 SL200H/DTC/DF) – manufactured by Dakshin India Pvt. Ltd. The esterification reaction was performed in a glass reactor of 50mL capacity and assembled with four baffles with a provision for stirring using three bladed turbine impeller. The flat bottom glass reactor was clamped in an ultrasonic water bath, nearly at a height of 2cm above the base as per the reported literature [16]. The ultrasound water bath ensures constant reaction temperature which can be maintained by heater or by circulating flow of cooling water. Besides, the ultrasound water bath is provided with transducers having longitudinal vibrations, operating at frequencies of 25kHz and 40kHz and maximum power rating of approximately 200W. Scheme 1 outlines the enzymatic synthesis of isobutyl propionate using enzyme lipase B in SFS. In a standard experimental procedure, a measured quantity of isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid in a fixed molar ratio were charged into the reactor vessel to make up reaction volume upto 15mL without any other solvent in the reaction mixture. On mild stirring, the reaction mixture formulated into a homogenous phase and the reaction was commenced by addition of immobilized lipase B as biocatalyst. The agitation speed, duty cycle, specified temperature of bath, power and frequency were maintained and the reaction was performed till acquirement of equilibrium condition. With the intention to monitor the progress of reaction, small aliquot of samples were withdrawn at a definite time interval, analysed and interpreted by standard acid value method in term of its acid value. At the lattermost of reaction, the reusability of immobilized lipase was determined. Reaction mixture was filtered through whattman filter paper to isolate the sample solution and used immobilized lipase B. The used enzyme was washed and rinsed several time with acetone to remove the traces of reactants or products, further dried at around 30°C–40°C and the recovered enzyme was kept in desiccator for 24h, prior to its use for the next run of esterification reaction.

The reaction mixture was filtered through whattman filter paper to separate the sample solution and lipase enzyme. The sample solution contained the desired product isobutyl propionate, trace amount of unreacted isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid. The unreacted propionic acid was neutralized by saturated sodium hydroxide solution. The unreacted isobutyl alcohol was eliminated by ethanol extraction [17].

The analysis of enzyme activity was followed from the methodology reported in literature [26]. The stock solution constitutes the mixture of 85.95mL n-heptane containing (0.16M), 1.35mL and (0.33M) 2.7mL butyric acid and butanol respectively. As per the reported literature studies, calculated amount of lipase enzyme was transferred in an Erlenmeyer flask containing 3mLof stock solution. The Erlenmeyer flask with stock solution was ascent to a raised surface of incubated shaker at 40°C, 50°C and 60°C for 1h at 150rpm speed. After 1h, 1mL of solution was quenched with ethanol and instantly titrated against 0.02M NaOH solution using phenolphthalein indicator. The blank reading of the reaction was determined in absence of enzyme. The blank reading and the titre reading investigated the relative activity of the enzyme. The esterification activity is given by,
(1)
Enzyme
activity
( Ea ) =
( Vo - V ) × M × 100
A × T
Where

ce:list-item.ce:para: V represents volume of the samples
M represents molarity of the NaOH
A represents amount of the enzyme
T represents time of reaction in minutes.

ce:section.ce:para:
The conversion of isobutyl propionate was expressed in terms of percent (%) conversion which was calculated in terms of acid value by titrating the unreacted propionic acid of the reaction mixture with 0.01N KOH using phenolphthalein indicator and quenching the sample solution by ethanol. All the samples were titrated thrice for accuracy. Percent conversion was calculated as follow:
(2)
Volume
of
KOH
utilised
( without
enzyme ) - Volume
of
KOH
utilised
( with
enzyme )
Volume
of
KOH
utilised
( without
enzyme )
× 100

The product was analysed by FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Miracle 10, and IR Affinity-1S) and transmission mode was used to analyze the IR spectra of samples. FT-IR spectra of product observed in a range of 500–4000cm−1 suggested the formation of ester. The carbonyl stretching of carboxylic group was clearly visible at around 1720.12cm−1, thereby confirming the formation of ester.

To estimate statistical parameters, single-factor – Analysis of variance (ANOVA) with excel was used. Statistical analysis is a significant mean to elucidate the experimental results. All experiments were performed in triplicate and data is depicted as mean±SD. P-values less than 0.5 are statistically substantial.

The fundamental kinetic characteristics are validated by the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km). The kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were determined by plotting Lineweaver-Burk plot and the initial rates were obtained by plotting concentration versus time graph [19]. Various models were tested on the basis of initial rate, namely, Random Bi-Bi, Ping-Pong Bi-Bi, Ordered Bi-Bi models.

Random Bi-Bi model, where the substrate bound to the enzyme in preferred order and products are also released in random order. The initial rate equation is given by:
(3)
v
0
=
V
max [ A ] [ B ]
[ A ] [ B ] +
K
B
[ A ] +
K
A
[ B ]
K
iA
K
B

Ping-Pong Bi-Bi model is characterized by the release of one or more products before all substrate binds to the enzyme. The initial rate equation is given by:
(4)
v
0
=
V
max [ A ] [ B ]
[ A ] [ B ] +
K
B
[ A ] + ( 1 + [ A ] /
K
iA
) +
K
A
[ B ] ( 1 + [ B ] /
K
iB
)

Ordered Bi-Bi model where, the mechanisms compulsorily follow the order of substrate addition to the enzyme and afford the release of two products in defined and sequence order. The mechanism is usually symmetrical. The initial rate equation is given by:
(5)
v
0
=
V
max [ A ] [ B ]
[ A ] [ B ] +
K
B
[ A ] +
K
A
[ B ] ( 1 + [ B ] /
K
iA
) +
K
iA
K
B
( 1 + [ B ] /
K
i
)

The kinetic models determined for the isobutyl propionate synthesis was studied on the basis of initial rate determination which is calculated by changing both the reactant concentrations methodically.

The reaction temperature study is a preeminent parameter for catalytic reaction which aids to improve the substrates solubility in reaction media, takes the edge off the viscosity of reaction medium, enhances substrate interaction and also molecular collision–interaction [6,21]. However, ultrasound irradiation increases the temperature which disrupts the ultrasound effect on the reaction medium. [16,18]. The high temperature of reaction medium reduces the surface tension and viscosity, disturbing the formation and successive collapse of bubbles [1]. As a consequence, the study on the effect of temperature is mandatory for any ultrasound assisted enzyme catalysed reaction to identify optimum reaction temperature. The enzymes being extremely sensitive appears to exhibit highest activity at appropriate temperature [19]. In order to evaluate the effect of temperature, the reactions were supervised at different operating temperatures ranging from 40°C to 70°C using enzyme lipase B in presence of ultrasound. Fig. 1 A illustrates that maximum equilibrium conversion of 93.01% is obtained at 60°C in 3h of reaction time. However, percentage conversion increased from 79.95% to 93.01% when temperature was raised from 40°C to 60°C. Fig. 1B shows that initial rate of reaction increased from 0.099M/min/g catalyst to 0.574M/min/g catalyst as the temperature increased from 40°C to 60°C but initial rate of reaction dropped at 70°C. At optimal low temperature, the bubble generation is low, yet they collapse with vigorous force which lead to boundless transfer of reactant molecule on the catalyst surface sites [21]. Comparable effects of temperature on esterification reactions in presence of ultrasound have been reported [6]. At 70°C, there is reduction in surface tension and viscosity, also the formation and upcoming collapse of bubbles is disturbed. This resulted in decreased cavitational intensity that reduced conversion after 3h to only 88.7%. Thus, a noteworthy rise in temperature was considered to be unfavorable as the transfer of reactant molecule to the catalyst surface was confined. The reduction in cavitational intensity at higher temperature above 60°C is responsible for negative impact on reaction yield. Other possibility for lower conversion is related to thermal denaturation of enzyme against heat which are usually considered to be most critical [20,21]. Thus, an optimal reaction temperature for the biocatalytic synthesis of isobutyl propionate was considered at 60°C and accounted for further reactions.

The influence of enzyme loading offers a significant importance for economic and environment friendly production of ester compounds [22]. The biotransformation reaction of isobutyl propionate was executed without use of any solvent and the excess enzyme particles present in the reaction mixture creates internal diffusion problem due to increase in viscosity and decrease in mass transfer efficiency. The cost of enzyme is a major concern in enzymatic reaction and its optimum use is always recommended to reduce the cost of the process [15,20]. Hence, it is significant to optimize the enzyme loading for the conversion of propionic acid into the corresponding ester. Thus, the effect of enzyme loading was studied ranging from 2.5% w/w to 5% w/w for higher conversion and economic profitability of isobutyl propionate synthesis. Fig. 2 A clearly indicates that a satisfactory conversion was attained at 4% w/w of enzyme loading in 3h reaction time. The percent conversion increased from 71.8% at 2.5% w/w of enzyme loading to 93.14% at 4% w/w of enzyme loading due to availability of more active site to enzymatic substrate [23]. At 5% w/w of enzyme loading the conversion obtained was around 94.01% in 3h. The conversion noted for high concentration of enzyme above 4% w/w depicted marginal rise in percent conversion. Similar relationship of conversion rate with catalyst loading was observed for the synthesis of ethyl-3-phenylpropanoate [24]. Fig. 2B shows that the initial rate of reaction (0.037M/min/g catalyst) is highest at 4% (w/w) of enzyme loading and initial rate of reaction decreases beyond 4% (w/w) enzyme loading. The reaction medium does not allow the excess amount of enzyme to take part in reaction [25]. Another possible reason may be agglomeration of immobilized enzyme and higher catalyst loading of enzyme may disrupts the solubility [26]. Hence 4% w/w of biocatalyst amount was considered as the optimal quantity and carried out for further experiments.

Various experimental studies were performed by varying the mole ratio of isobutanol and propionic acid to optimize it for esterification reaction in SFS. The reactions were conducted by varying the molar ratio of propionic acid to isobutanol from 1:1 to 1:4 (1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4) and the reaction volume was retained at 15mL. Fig. 3 depicts the outcome of the influence of reactant concentration on isobutyl propionate synthesis. The maximum conversion of 94.23% was observed at 1:3 propionic acid to isobutanol alcohol ratio. The percentage conversion was increased from 78.8% to 94.23% when molar ratio was changed from 1:1 to 1:3. As the amount of alcohol increases, the viscosity of reaction mixture decreases leading to enhanced cavitation effect. At this stage, surface tension of reaction mixture increases causing scattering of molecules and easy transport of reactant molecules to active catalytic site, thereby enhancing the percentage conversion [27]. As the esterification reaction is reversible, the overrun of the nucleophilic concentration may push the reaction in forward direction for completion [28]. Similar effect was also shown for isoamyl acetate [3], isoamyl myristate [29]. While further increase in concentration of alcohol beyond this point (1:4) showed marginal change in the percent conversion.
The marginal drop in the conversion may be due to the presence of hydrophobic interaction of tail of alcohol with hydrophobic compartment of enzyme [2,21]. The oversupply of alcohol in the reaction medium binds it with enzyme and impedes active sites of enzyme. This may cause conformational changes to enzyme and form the dead-end inhibition complex [3,26]. Also at 1:4 acid to alcohol ratio, considerable drop in conversion was observed practicably due to dilution of acid with alcohol which resulted to lower collision frequentness with enzyme. The inhibitory action of alcohol and marginal decrease in conversion can be reasoned for increased cavitation giving rise to micro turbulence and emulsification. The maximum optimum conversion obtained at 1:3 acid to alcohol ratio was noticed due to extremity of micro turbulence induced by cavitation bubble due to decreased viscosity of alcohol [30,31].

Here, agitation is also used along with ultrasound as agitation helps to keep the solid enzyme in suspended form as well as to reduce the external mass transfer resistance. In the heterogeneous reaction system, both the reactants were present in liquid form and the biocatalyst was present in solid form [2]. The optimal agitation is required for heterogeneous system by delivering constant mixing of reagents and to improve the mass transfer of reacting molecules to active catalyst surface [23]. Thus, experiments were performed by varying the agitation speed from 0rpm to 200rpm. Fig. 4A illustrates that the conversion gradually increased from 40.12% to 93.05% as the speed of agitation increased from 0rpm to 150rpm respectively. The sole use of ultrasound irradiation, without stirring (0rpm) gave a poor conversion of 40.12%. As seen from Fig. 4B, initial rate of reaction increases from 0.0091M/min/g catalyst to 0.068M/min/g catalyst when agitation speed was increased from 0rpm to 150rpm. The physical turbulence created by ultrasound due to only cavitation was not enough to suspend the enzyme and a sluggish conversion was achieved [26,32]. This indicates that an optimum agitation speed of 150rpm along with ultrasonication has intense effect on conversion rates and is required to surpass the external mass transfer resistance [33–35]. The combination of cavitation effect of ultrasound and a small amount of agitation is effective in esterification reaction in order to achieve efficient improvement in conversion rates [23].

The influence of duty cycle on esterification reaction was studied by varying the ON-OFF time span of ultrasonic irradiation. Duty cycle plays an important role in ultrasound assisted esterification reactions and it is suggested to carry out synthesis upto a favorable value. Herein, we studied the effect of duty cycle on conversion rate by varying it at 25% (5min on &15min off), 50% (10min on &10min off) and 75% (15min on and 5min off) and the results are depicted in Fig. 5A. As can be seen in the Fig. 5A that increasing the percentage duty cycle from 25% to 50%, the equilibrium conversion was increased from 83.92% to 93.12%, while further increase in duty cycle beyond 50% leads to decrease in percentage conversion [23]. Fig. 5B shows an increase in initial rate of reaction till 0.011M/min/g catalyst for 50% duty cycle. The conversion increased up to an optimum functioning of ‘ON’ time and ‘OFF’ time of ultrasonic waves [8]. The prolonged exposure of ultrasonic irradiation to the reaction medium may possibly cause degradation of enzyme, resulting in decrease in conversion [30]. Moreover, uninterrupted performance without pulse can damage the transducers used for ultrasound [32,36]. For economical synthesis, a lower duty cycle is recommended to carry out esterification [37]. Hence, a duty cycle of 50% was used for further sets of experiments.

Ultrasound intensity in sonochemical reactions is an important parameter for efficient conversion rate and also for economy of the process [2,39]. In order to evaluate the influence of ultrasound intensity in isobutyl propionate synthesis, experiments were performed by varying the ultrasound power at 30W, 40W and 60W. The dissipated power calculated by calorimetric studies was 23.2W, 30W and 40W for different input powers of 30W, 40W and 60W respectively. The pressure amplitude for the corresponding calculated dissipated power was around 0.28bar, 0.44bar and 0.52bar respectively. Thus, power dissipation increases with an increase in power and a direct relation between power dissipation and power is observed. The results for the different ultrasound power are plotted in Fig. 6A. It is noticed that as the input power increases from 30W to 40W, corresponding increase in conversion is obtained, further increase in input power (60W) results in lower conversion. Fig. 6B indicates that initial rate of reaction increases till 0.0608M/min/g catalyst at 40W power but initial rate of reaction decreases to 0.03601M/min/g catalyst with further increase in power (60W). The severe effect of sonication due to high input power may cause enzyme deactivation or confirmation change in enzyme accounting poor conversion [7,10]. Besides, as the input power increases, energy dissipation increases which further increases cavitation intensity. The profound effect of cavitation leads to the formation of large quantity of ultrasound cavitation bubbles at a high rate that subsequently implodes and generates shock waves. This gives more turbulence and thus higher interfacial area that results in excellent emulsification between two phases of isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid thereby enhancing the rate of reaction for better conversion [8,38]. Moreover, further increase in ultrasound power causes robust turbulence, which attributes to enzyme deactivation and likely reduction in catalytic activity resulting in lower conversion and slow rate of reaction. It is also to be noted that there is a possibility of physical loss of enzyme at very high turbulence. Thus, input power of 40W was employed to be optimum for further studies.

After the optimization of power, similar experiments were carried out to optimize the ultrasound frequency. Synthesis of isobutyl propionate was studied at two ultrasonic frequencies such as 25kHz and 40kHz under constant optimized condition. From Fig. 6C it is evident that irradiation at ultrasound frequency 25kHz showed higher conversion as compared to 40kHz. The calorimetric studies were carried out and the power dissipation for 25kHz and 40kHz at 60W power was found to be 42W and 30W respectively. Higher power is dissipated at lower ultrasound frequency that causes rise in the temperature and pressure in the operation phase [40]. This effect overcomes the mass transfer limitation and signifies efficient increase in percent conversion [39]. Also, higher frequency has upheld to hamper the catalytic activity and following denaturation of enzyme [26]. Similar studies have been performed that reported 25kHz as the optimum frequency for enzyme catalyzed reaction [35].

For the economic profitability of bio-catalysed esterification reaction, it is suggested to evaluate the enzyme reusability studies. After completion of each cycle (3h), the immobilized lipase enzyme was separated from reaction mixture by filtration followed by washing with acetone and cooling overnight in desiccator. The decline in the activity of enzyme was observed as seen in Fig. 7 with continuous use in batch studies. For the study performed with optimized parameters, a marginal decrease in conversion was observed upto seventh cycle, due to loss in catalytic activity of enzyme. The decline in activity of the enzyme can also be attributed to loss of water from the environment of enzyme particle or inhibition by substrate and product [40]. In the present study, the ultrasound assisted enzyme catalysed esterification reaction reached equilibrium in 3h time span. In this reaction, the lower conversion can be reasoned due to denaturation of enzyme by propionic acid due to prolonged and continuous exposure of enzyme to ultrasonication. This may also lead to conformational changes of lipase structure [29,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of ultrasonic irradiation v/s conventional stirring method
ce:section.ce:para: Isobutyl propionate synthesis was studied using three conditions i.e., conventional stirring method, ultrasonic irradiation, ultrasonic irradiation without stirring (Fig. 8). As seen in Table 1 it was observed that ultrasound assisted synthesis in SFS gave a maximum conversion of 95.14% in only 3h under optimized parameters. Ultrasonic irradiation method without stirring gave a sluggish conversion of 30.6% in 3h reaction time. Whereas, in conventional stirring method (without ultrasound), the reaction rate was comparatively slow and the process time attained to reach equilibrium was 10h to give 92.5% conversion. The enzyme loading was optimized to 4% w/w under ultrasonication as compared to traditional stirring method (5% w/w). Use of ultrasound also significantly reduced the stirring speed from 300rpm to 150rpm. The most appropriate effect of ultrasound irradiation in heterogeneous system can be imputed to cavitation. The mass transfer effect and formation of microjets is the reason for inherent rate improvement. The cavitation effect increases the temperature and pressure and also leads to shock waves causing microscopic turbulence. Consequently, these effects increase the diffusion of reactant molecules on the active catalytic surface sites and significantly enhances the mass transfer and conversion. In traditional method, mechanical stirring solely contributes for mixing. In heterogeneous system, the rate determining step is the mass transfer at the interface between two phases. The specific effect is the unproportional collapse near a solid surface which forms microjets. This effect is responsible for augmentation of reaction rates [42]. The inflated energy level of bubble cavitation under ultrasound can promote particle fragmentation and molecular excitation, increasing the surface obliged mass transfer [43]. Therefore, ultrasonication was instrumental in increasing the conversion and reducing the process time to reach equilibrium for isobutyl propionate synthesis by vanquishing the mass transfer limitation and improving the rate of reaction. Table 2 demonstrates the range of reaction condition studied and resultant optimized parameters.

The kinetic model were proposed for esterification of propionic acid with isobutyl alcohol by using different bi-substrate models namely, Random bi-bi mechanism, Ping-Pong bi-bi mechanism, ordered bi-bi mechanism. The construction of kinetic modeling for synthesis was based on determination of initial rate of reaction [15,21]. The results represents that both the substrates can be varied and cause inhibition for this esterification reaction. The initial rates are calculated by varying both the reactant concentration alternatively, since the experiments are performed in solvent free system SFS.

In the literature, the diverse models have been proposed and reported for the enzyme catalysed reactions [8,19]. In this study, the acceptable data obtained have been investigated for three different mechanisms. In order to obtain accuracy and precision non-linear regression and minimization of sum of square of errors (SSE) were incorporated and applied using Microsoft excel 2010 and model variables were calculated.

The experimental and predicted rates have been reported in Fig. 9A. The initial rate of esterification reaction can be determined from data of reaction time and plotted against acid concentration. The initial rate of reaction depicted from Fig. 9B shows that initial rate increases with an increase in the acid concentration from 2.24M to 3.85M and decreased at 5.982M. The initial rate reaches to highest value 0.103M/min/g catalyst from 0.025M/min/g catalyst and again start dropping which can be validated to inhibition by acid at high concentration. It is well noticed from Table 3 that kinetic variables evaluated by ordered bi-bi and random bi-bi models are non-significant; hence are redundant. The Ping-Pong bi-bi mechanism with inhibition by both propionic acid and isobutyl alcohol exhibit the ideal fit. The Ping-Pong bi-bi mechanism also called double displacement reaction signifies the release of one or more products before all substrates cohere to the enzyme. The mechanism of inhibition by both substrates is depicted below in eqn.
(6)
E =

⇔
K
A
E ·

⇔

⇔

Q
E + Q

The initial rate equation is given as follows:
(7)

0
=
V
max [ A ] [ B ]

K
iB
)
Mechanistic study:

Kinetically controlled synthesis is an excelling approach which leads to an enhanced rate of reaction and better percentage yield compared to thermodynamically controlled synthesis. The present work is performed in absence of solvent (SFS), thus the substrate concentration is present in large concentration in order to dilute the total reaction mixture indication no diffusion barriers. The reaction is kinetically controlled instead of diffusion controlled. The esterification of isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid catalysed by immobilized lipase include two probabilities where the enzyme lipase react with isobutyl alcohol to provide dead-end lipase isobutanol intermediate. While another probability include, where lipase enzyme promptly react with propionic acid to yield efficient lipase propionic acid intermediate. However in the present study, if the isobutyl alcohol and lipase complex (acyl-lipase intermediate) react with propionic acid forming dead-end intermediate will cause inhibition [15]. Furthermore, after extended time of reaction (3h), the yield of the desired product was constant, proving that the reaction was carried out in forward direction [19]. The current work explains the modeling of lipase catalyzed esterification by the Ping-Pong bi-bi mechanism. The esterification studies have documented only the inhibition by alcohol for reaction with long chain acids in literature. This work deals with the inhibition of alcohol as well as propionic acid, which is a short chain acid [44,45]. Ping-Pong bi-bi mechanism proposed that propionic acid reacts with immobilized lipase to form propionic acid-lipase complex which isomerizes into acyl-lipase intermediate and further release water as the first product. Later on isobutyl alcohol combines with acyl-lipase complex to form binary complex, which isomerizes to corresponding alcohol propionate-lipase complex. Finally binary complex of alcohol propionate-lipase cleaves and break down into the desired propionate ester and lipase [15]. The binary complex reaction mechanism is schematically represented in Fig. 10 . Lipase catalyzed synthesis of isobutyl propionate follows first order reaction. This reaction is dependent upon the concentration of both isobutyl alcohol and propionic acid, but one of the components is present in large excess and thus its concentration hardly changes with the progress of the reaction. So, isobutyl alcohol is in large excess and the concentration of alcohol is very high as compared to that of propionic acid. Here, the reaction is considered to be first order reaction with respect to propionic acid.

The Ping-Pong bi-bi kinetic model showed an ideal fit with the experimental data with following parameters Vmax
=50.00M/min/g catalyst, KA
=4.87×10−2 M, KB
=9.06×10−4 M, KiA
=9.8×10−1 M, KiB
=1.05×10−3 M & SSE =2.74×10−4. The kinetic parameters possess their individual physical importance i.e.,Vmax is the maximum initial rate of esterification reaction and the Vmax value is higher in case of ultrasound assisted reaction (Vmax
=50.00M/min/g catalyst) compared to conventional method which is 0.5M/min/g catalyst suggesting greater tendency for lipase substrate complex to break down into products. The high ratio of Ka/Kb suggests the higher affinity of substrate for enzyme. SSE evaluates the difference between the data values and approximate model values. Statistical parameters demonstrate smaller SSE indicating agreeable fit of the model [46,47]. Here, the SSE evaluated for random bi-bi model and ordered bi-bi model was higher i.e., 8.9×10−4 and 4.5×10−4 than Ping-Pong bi-bi model i.e., 2.7×10−4. A parity plot was formulated between theoretical rate v/s experimental rates as seen in Fig. 9A. From the plot, it is observed that Ping-Pong bi-bi model showed appropriate fit.

The ultrasound energy was successfully employed for the potential biocatalytic synthesis of isobutyl propionate, rum flavor using lipase Fermase CALB™10000 in a SFS. The impact of parameters was studied successfully. After purification steps, maximum conversion of 95.14% was attained in 3h at optimized reaction parameters. Time required was much less as compared to conventional method. The immobilized lipase enzyme offered recyclability upto seven cycles under optimum conditions. Thus, this developed protocol shows application of ultrasound irradiation for better conversion in proportionately shorter span of time. Subsequent reduction in agitation speed and amount of catalyst is also observed. The bisubstrate kinetic model was proposed to the experimental data and showed that biocatalytic reaction of isobutanol with propionic acid followed Ping-Pong bi-bi model with inhibition by both alcohol and acid. Amongst three preferred models, there was an excellent agreement with Ping-Pong bi-bi model with inhibition by both alcohol and acid showing better fitting. The refined protocol recommend superior biocatalytic activity using ultrasound irradiation which is environmentally safe signifying wide applicability in various industries.
